statement: Professional qualification recognition under the EEA is governed by Directive 2005/36/EC (as amended), which Iceland has transposed. However, in practice, recognition of qualifications for regulated professions (doctors, nurses, architects, engineers) obtained outside the EU/EEA can be slower and more complex for EEA states than for EU members. Iceland's Medical Directorate reported average processing times of 6–9 months for non-Icelandic medical degree recognition in 2025, compared to 3–4 months in comparable EU countries. The European Professional Card (EPC) system, which simplifies recognition for selected professions, is available in EU states but not yet fully operational for EEA countries.
source_name: Ministry of Education — Regulated professions registry; European Commission — Regulated professions database
caveats: Processing times are averages and vary by profession and country of qualification. Some delays in Iceland reflect the small size of assessment bodies rather than legal barriers. The EPC system is limited to a few professions (nurses, pharmacists, physiotherapists, mountain guides, real estate agents).

related_entries.statement: Iceland faces persistent skills shortages in healthcare, construction, and technology. The healthcare sector had approximately 800 unfilled positions in 2025, with particular shortages in nursing, psychiatry, and geriatric care. Construction labour shortages have been estimated at 2,000–3,000 workers during peak building periods. The IT sector reports roughly 1,500 unfilled technical positions. EU membership would not change free movement rights (already guaranteed under EEA) but could improve mutual recognition of professional qualifications, particularly for regulated professions like medicine and engineering where EEA recognition processes are slower than intra-EU equivalents.

Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.
